title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array I

statement.markdown: Suppose a sorted array is rotated at some pivot unknown to you beforehand. Given the rotated sorted array, return the index of the target if it is present in the array; otherwise, return -1. You must write an algorithm with a runtime of O(log n), where n is the number of elements in the array. The array is guaranteed to not contain duplicate elements.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer n, which is the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains n integers describing the elements of the array, separated by spaces.
The third line contains an integer, which is the target value to be searched in the array.
statement.outputFormat: On a single line, print the index of the target element if it exists in the array; otherwise, print -1.

constraints.description: The number of elements in the array
The target value to be searched

examples.explanation: The target value 0 is present at index 4 in the rotated sorted array.
The target value 0 is not present in the array.

hints.text: Consider using a modified binary search approach to solve this problem. The array is sorted and rotated, so you can take advantage of this property to reduce the search space.
Think about how you can use the midpoint of the array to determine which half of the array is sorted. This will help you to discard one half of the array in each iteration.

Use the fact that the array is rotated to your advantage. If the pivot element is at the midpoint, you can determine which half of the array is sorted and which is not. This will help you to narrow down the search space.

Consider the relationship between the target value and the pivot element. If the target is less than the pivot, you can discard the right half of the array. If the target is greater than the pivot, you can discard the left half of the array.

The core technique to solve this problem is to use a modified binary search that takes advantage of the fact that the array is sorted and rotated. By using the midpoint to determine which half of the array is sorted and which is not, you can reduce the search space and find the target value in O(log n) time.

editorial.title: Efficient Search in Rotated Sorted Array
editorial.intuition: The problem involves searching for a target value in a rotated sorted array, which can be solved using a modified binary search approach. This is because the array is sorted in a specific way, allowing us to make educated guesses about the target's location.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would involve checking each element in the array until the target is found, resulting in a time complexity of O(n). However, this approach is inefficient and does not take advantage of the array's sorted nature.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach would be to use a binary search algorithm, but with modifications to account for the array's rotation. This would involve checking the middle element and determining which half of the array to continue searching in.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a modified binary search algorithm that takes into account the rotation of the array. By checking the middle element and comparing it to the target, we can determine which half of the array to continue searching in, resulting in a time complexity of O(log n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the algorithm correctly handles all possible cases, including when the target is in the left or right half of the array, and when the array is rotated. This can be done by using a combination of mathematical induction and case analysis.
editorial.dryRun: To test the optimal solution, we can perform a dry run with a sample input, such as the array [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2] and the target value 0. By walking through the algorithm step-by-step, we can verify that it correctly returns the index of the target value.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the target value is not in the array
Not correctly determining which half of the array to continue searching in
Not accounting for the rotation of the array
Using a brute force approach instead of a modified binary search
Not correctly implementing the binary search algorithm

functionSignature.java: public int search(int[] nums, int target);
functionSignature.python: def search(self, nums: list[int], target: int) -> int:
